coredata.dc:title: Multicolor lignin-derived carbon quantum dots: Controllable synthesis and photocatalytic applications
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Lingli
Wu, Hao
Xie, Shengyu
Yang, Hongmin
Shen, Dekui
coredata.dc:description:
The cost-effective, high-performance fabrication of multicolor carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from biomass is critical to its prospective application. Lignin, with its phenylpropane structural unit and abundant functional groups, is a reliable precursor for the formation of high-quality CQDs. Here, CQDs from renewable lignin were successfully prepared via a two-step method, and controllable preparation of CQDs to emit blue, green, yellow, and red fluorescence was achieved by adjusting the N-containing acid dopant. The investigated fluorescence mechanism revealed that the energy bandgap of the lignin-derived CQDs decreased and the emission wavelength redshifted as the graphitization degree and number of C=O surface groups increased. The light absorption region of the Bi7O9I3 photocatalysts modified with blue CQDs to red CQDs gradually broadened in the redshifted direction. In particular, compared with pure Bi7O9I3 (70.9%), the red CQDs-modified Bi7O9I3 (RCQD/BOI) can remove 94.7% of antibiotics within 60 min of light irradiation. The superior photocatalytic activity of RCQD/BOI is attributed to the established internal electric field that accelerates charge transfer and effectively separates the photogenerated e+-h+ inside Bi7O9I3. Furthermore, through radical capture, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiments validated the photocatalytic mechanism of RCQD/BOI and revealed that h+,
O2 − and
OH are key reactants in the photocatalytic degradation process. This promising and sustainable approach for the synthesis of multicolor lignin-derived CQDs opens the avenue of high-value utilization of biomass resources, green production of CQDs nanomaterials, and efficient development of CQDs-based photocatalysts for wastewater treatment.

head.ce:title: Multicolor lignin-derived carbon quantum dots: Controllable synthesis and photocatalytic applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The cost-effective, high-performance fabrication of multicolor carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from biomass is critical to its prospective application. Lignin, with its phenylpropane structural unit and abundant functional groups, is a reliable precursor for the formation of high-quality CQDs. Here, CQDs from renewable lignin were successfully prepared via a two-step method, and controllable preparation of CQDs to emit blue, green, yellow, and red fluorescence was achieved by adjusting the N-containing acid dopant. The investigated fluorescence mechanism revealed that the energy bandgap of the lignin-derived CQDs decreased and the emission wavelength redshifted as the graphitization degree and number of C=O surface groups increased. The light absorption region of the Bi7O9I3 photocatalysts modified with blue CQDs to red CQDs gradually broadened in the redshifted direction. In particular, compared with pure Bi7O9I3 (70.9%), the red CQDs-modified Bi7O9I3 (RCQD/BOI) can remove 94.7% of antibiotics within 60 min of light irradiation. The superior photocatalytic activity of RCQD/BOI is attributed to the established internal electric field that accelerates charge transfer and effectively separates the photogenerated e+-h+ inside Bi7O9I3. Furthermore, through radical capture, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiments validated the photocatalytic mechanism of RCQD/BOI and revealed that h+,
O2 − and
OH are key reactants in the photocatalytic degradation process. This promising and sustainable approach for the synthesis of multicolor lignin-derived CQDs opens the avenue of high-value utilization of biomass resources, green production of CQDs nanomaterials, and efficient development of CQDs-based photocatalysts for wastewater treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Lignin, an essential organic component of biomass (with a content of approximately 10–35%), is also the most abundant naturally productive aromatic oxygen-containing polymer in nature [1,2]. The utilization of lignin resources mainly focuses on traditional combustion and heating and the high-value conversion of liquid chemicals and carbon nanomaterials. In recent years, the use of lignin (biomass) organic solid waste to produce carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon nano-onions (CNOs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and other nanomaterials has become a research hotspot in this field [3]. In particular, CQDs not only are chemically resistant, environmentally friendly, photoluminescent, tunable, and easily modified but also have good water dispersibility, biocompatibility, nontoxicity, etc., and can be widely applied in the fields of biomedicine, energy storage, electronic devices, and catalysis [4–7]. Most of the reported syntheses of CQDs use more expensive carbon nanomaterials (CNTs) or toxic carbon sources (polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons), which pose severe harm to ecosystems [8]. Using low-cost and environmentally friendly lignin as a carbon source not only provides a new pathway for the green synthesis and low-cost preparation of CQDs but also contributes to the high-value utilization of lignin.

The preparation of high-performance CQDs is the key to their wide application, and heteroatom doping is currently the most effective means to modulate the optical properties of CQDs. Zhao et al. used glucose as a carbon source to modulate the size and degree of oxidation of the sp2 region of CQDs by varying the concentration of concentrated HCl, realizing the emission of CQDs at the full wavelength of the visible luminescence region [9]. Zheng et al. prepared blue, green, orange, and red CQDs with bright fluorescence by regulating the mixing ratio of asparagine and p-phenylenediamine [10]. Wang et al. prepared N and S codoped white CQDs using ethanol and H2SO4 and isolated blue, green, and yellow CQDs by column chromatography [11]. Inspired by studies on the modulation of the optical properties of small molecule-based CQDs, Rani et al. hydrothermally prepared N-doped blue CQDs from extracted lignin and urea with an optimal emission wavelength of 447 nm [12]. Zhang et al. also hydrothermally prepared N-doped green CQDs from alkali lignin and ethylenediamine with an optimal emission wavelength of 520 nm [13]. Myint et al. hydrothermally prepared N,S-codoped green CQDs from Klaxon lignin and concentrated H2SO4/HNO3 with an optimum emission wavelength of 560 nm [14]. The reported lignin-derived CQDs are limited to emitting short-wavelength blue–green light, while insufficient studies have been conducted on lignin-derived CQDs with long-wavelength yellow and red light emission.

Photocatalysis is considered the most promising technology for water pollution treatment[15]. Recently, researchers have made rapid progress in developing CQDs-modified semiconductor materials[16,17]. CQDs, as novel 0D quasispherical carbon nanomaterials of less than 10 nm, can not only tune the band structure to broaden the absorption edge but also act as an electron acceptor or buffer to transfer charge to the surface, thus inhibiting rapid electron–hole recombination [18–21]. Although there have been limited reports on composites such as CQDs/BiOCl [22], CQDs/BiOBr [23,24], and CQDs/BiSbO4 [25], there is an urgent need for an in-depth investigation of the impacts of the optical properties of CQDs on the photocatalytic performance of composites.

Therefore, a series of lignin-derived CQDs were synthesized through a two-step process involving an acidolysis step followed by a hydrothermal treatment. By adjusting the N-containing acid dopant, blue, green, yellow, and red fluorescence-emitting CQDs were successfully prepared. The conformational relationship between the physicochemical structure of lignin-derived CQDs and their optical properties was investigated to reveal the fluorescence mechanism of multicolor CQDs. Additionally, these CQDs-modified Bi7O9I3 composites were successfully applied for the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants. The role of the CQDs in enhancing the photocatalytic performance of the composites and the photocatalytic reaction mechanism were elucidated. This work proposes a novel strategy for the synthesis of multicolor lignin-derived CQDs, enabling high-value utilization of lignin resources and sustainable application of CQDs nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and CQDs modified composites

ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of CQDs derived from lignin was carried out by a two-step hydrothermal method, as described in Fig. 1 . Briefly, 0.3 g of the designed acid and 0.3 g of lignin were slowly added to a beaker containing 30 mL of deionized water and constantly stirred to make the mixture homogeneous. The lignin was reacted in an acid solution at 90 °C for 60 min and stirred continuously at a rate of 350 rpm. The cooled solution was then vacuum filtered (0.8 μm). Next, the resulting filtrate A was moved to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and hydrothermally reacted at 200 °C for 12 h. The reacted solution was naturally cooled to room temperature and vacuum filtered (0.22 μm). The obtained filtrate B was dialyzed in a 3000 Da dialysis bag in deionized water to remove unreacted small molecules, and the deionized water was changed every 6 h until the solution outside the bag was clarified. The dialyzed CQDs solution was freeze-dried at −60 °C for 48 h, and finally, after which the obtained CQDs powder was collected. The types of acid solvents employed were benzoic acid, 2-aminobenzenesulfonic acid, 2, 4-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid, and 1, 2, 4-triaminobenzene dihydrochloride, which yielded four lignin-derived CQDs, denoted as B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and R-CQDs, respectively.

CQDs modified Bi7O9I3 composites were synthesized via a hydrothermal method. 4.368 g of Bi(NO3)3⋅5H2O was added to 120 mL of EG and sonicated for 0.5 h to obtain solution I. Then, 0.026 g of the designated CQDs and 1.494 g of KI powder were added to 120 mL of EG and sonicated for 0.5 h to obtain solution II. Solution I was then gradually mixed with solution II and stirred for 2 h. The mixtures were moved to a 300 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 160 °C for 24 h. Then the obtained solution was centrifuged and washed. The CQDs/Bi7O9I3 powders were finally obtained by drying at 60 °C overnight. The B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and R-CQDs modified Bi7O9I3 composites were denoted as BCQD/BOI、GCQD/BOI、YCQD/BOI and RCQD/BOI, respectively. The pure Bi7O9I3 (BOI) photocatalyst was acquired by a procedure similar to that described above without CQDs.
The characterization of the composite photocatalysts is detailed in the Supporting Information.

The photocatalytic performance of the samples was tested by degrading an antibiotic (TC, tetracycline hydrochloride) under the irradiation of a 300 W Xe lamp. Briefly, 50 mg of the sample was placed in 200 ml of a 20 mg. L-1 TC solution with steady stirring in the dark for 0.5 h. The photocatalytic degradation reaction was then performed under simulated sunlight irradiation. The different reaction solutions were removed at certain time intervals and vacuum filtered (0.22 μm). A UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Yuanxi 5200) was used to determine the respective concentration of the TC solution at an absorption wavelength of 357 nm. The recyclability of the CQD/BOI composites was assessed by four consecutive cycling degradation tests. The degradation efficiency and active species analysis are detailed in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of multicolor CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological characteristics of the multicolor lignin-derived CQDs were observed using TEM and HR-TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a-d, the TEM images at the microscale of 20 nm clearly show that the four CQDs exhibit zero-dimensional quasispherical particles with sizes less than 10 nm, which are well dispersed and uniform. HR-TEM images further magnified by a factor of 10 show that the nucleus of all four CQDs has a lattice spacing of 0.32 nm inside the nucleus, corresponding to the crystalline structure of graphitic carbon (002) [26,27]. In particular, the R-CQDs have high-resolution lattice stripes. The average particle sizes of the B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQD, and R-CQDs gradually increase in the order of 3.2, 4.3, 4.9 and 5.nm, respectively. The zeta potentials of the four CQDs decreased in the order of 38.0 (B-CQDs) > 22.9 (G-CQDs) > 18.7 (Y-CQDs) > 17.1 (R-CQDs), suggesting reduced dispersion stability and a tendency toward aggregation.

The structures of the four lignin-derived CQDs were analysed, as shown in Fig. 3 a. The Raman spectra of all four CQDs exhibit a distinct G peak at 1578–1588 cm−1 and a D peak at 1348–1360 cm−1, which are attributed to sp2 graphitic carbon within the carbon core of the CQDs and sp3 amorphous carbon with surface defects, respectively [28,29]. The ratios of the intensity of the G peak to that of the D peak (IG /ID ) of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and R-CQDs are 1.06, 1.12, 1.28, and 1.55, respectively. This represents the increasing graphitization degree of the four CQDs in order, with the RCQDs featuring the highest graphitization degree within their carbon cores. As shown in Fig. 3b, the MALDI-TOF MS spectra present the molecular weight distributions of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and R-CQDs with increasing number average molecular weights (Mn) of 363.8, 380.6, 591.4, and 644.2, respectively. The mass spectra of the four CQDs also show multiple peaks separated by 18n (n is an integer) units, which is consistent with the relative molecular mass of nH2O. This suggests that the conjugated graphite structures within the carbon cores of the CQDs are formed by dehydration and condensation of the nonconjugated precursors [9]. As shown in Fig. 3c, the surface structures of the four CQDs were characterized by FT-IR, and the G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and R-CQDs clearly had more abundant surface functional groups than did the B-CQDs. The wide peaks at 3000–3652 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of the O–H/N–H groups, while the peaks at 2610–2940, 1763, 1405–1600, 1396, and 1120 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibrations of the C–H, C = O, C = C, C-N, and C-O groups, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3d, the XPS survey spectra of the four CQDs show distinct peaks at 284.8 eV (C1s), 532.3 eV (O1s) and 400.1 eV (N1s) [30–32]. This demonstrates that the surface of the B-CQDs primarily consists of C and O, while the G-CQDs, Y-CQDs and R-CQDs primarily consist of C, O, and N, respectively, and are N-doped lignin-derived CQDs.

The surface chemical composition of the CQDs was further analysed using high-resolution XPS. As shown in Fig. 4 a, C 1 s can be fitted to three peaks at 288.8, 286.4, and 284.8 eV, corresponding to C = O, sp3 carbon (C-O/C-N), and sp2 carbon (C–C/C = C), respectively. As shown in Fig. 4b, O 1 s can be fitted to two peaks at 533.4 and 532.0 eV, corresponding to C-O-C/C-OH and C = O, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4c, N 1 s can be fitted to two peaks at 401.1 and 399.4 eV, corresponding to graphitic nitrogen (C-N) and amide nitrogen (N–H), respectively [11,30,33,34]. Qualitatively, from B-CQDs to R-CQDs, the peak intensities of C–C/C = C, C = O, and C-N tend to increase sequentially. The high-resolution S 2p spectra of G-CQDs and Y-CQDs can be fitted to three deconvolution peaks at approximately 168.5 169.6 and 170.5 eV, corresponding to −C-SOx- (x = 2, 3, 4) originating from sulfonate in the dopants (Fig. S1). Based on the above characterization results, it could be inferred that these four lignin-derived CQDs are mainly composed of carbon cores with sp2-conjugated structures and surface functional groups containing O and N. The quantitative XPS analysis data for the four lignin-derived CQDs are listed in Table 1 . The C contents of the B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and R-CQDs were 62.52%, 63.14%, 67.21%, and 69.76%, respectively, also indicating an increase in the graphitization degree of the CQDs. The O content of the CQDs decreases from 37.48% to 25.81%, while the C = O content increases from 15.74% to 22.11% from the B-CQDs to the R-CQDs. The C–N contents of the four CQDs were 0.00%, 3.15%, 2.98%, and 2.84%, respectively. According to previous reports, the degree of graphitization and the presence of C = O and C–N groups, which involve emission centers, optical energy band gaps, and QYs, are the key factors affecting the optical properties of CQDs [9,10,35,36].

The absorption ability, excitation-emission centers, and fluorescence lifetimes of multicolor lignin-derived CQDs were characterized and analysed using UV–vis, PL, and TRPL. As shown in Fig. 5 a, aqueous solutions of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and R-CQDs emit bright blue, green, yellow, and red fluorescence, respectively, under 365 nm UV light. The UV–vis absorption spectra of all four CQDs show an absorption peak in the 200–300 nm range, attributed to the π-π* transition of C=C in the carbon core. The enhanced absorption intensity from B-CQDs to R-CQDs in the range of 200–300 nm indicates that the number of sp2-conjugated domains in the carbon core increases successively. Similarly, Y-CQDs and R-CQDs display a gradual increase in absorption intensity over the 400–600 nm wavelength range, which are assigned to the n-π* transitions of C = O on the CQDs surface [37]. The absorption edges of the B-CQDs and G-CQDs are ∼ 400 and 450 nm, respectively, while the absorption edges of the Y-CQDs and R-CQDs are approximately 600 and 650 nm, respectively. The improved light absorption in the visible region facilitates the long-wavelength emission of CQDs [11]. The PL spectra of the B-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths show almost the same peak positions in the emission spectra, indicating the excitation-independent behavior of the B-CQDs. The excitation-independent behavior is usually associated with fewer surface defects. The emission peaks of G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and R-CQDs redshift by 80, 95, and 110 nm, respectively, as the excitation wavelength increases, exhibiting gradually enhanced excitation-dependent behavior. As shown in Fig. 5b, the maximum emission peaks of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and R-CQDs under excitation wavelengths of 325, 350, 450, and 490 nm are centered at 400, 475, 520, and 610 nm, corresponding to the blue, green, yellow, and red PL centers, respectively. The Eg values calculated for the four CQDs were 3.14, 2.65, 2.21, and 1.93 eV, which are in accordance with the redshift results of the PL spectra. The relative fluorescence QYs calculated for the four CQDs were 10.8%, 23.6%, 21.8%, and 20.5%, which are similar to the variation tendencies of graphitic nitrogen levels in the CQDs.

As shown in Fig. 5c, the TRPL decay curves of the four CQDs show a similar biexponential fitting model, and the decay time increases sequentially. The specific fitting parameters for the TRPL decay curves of the four CQDs are listed in Table 2 , including the τ 1 component associated with the eigenstates and the τ 2 component associated with the surface defect states [38,39]. The former originates from π-π* transitions in the sp2 conjugated domains within the carbon nuclei of CQDs, while the latter results from n-π* transitions of O- or N-containing functional groups on the surface. The average lifetimes (τ avg) of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and R-CQDs were 3.37, 5.28, 5.90, and 7.61, respectively, which show a sequential increasing tendency. The A 1 values of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and R-CQDs were 91.52%, 72.31%, 71.20%, and 70.01%, respectively, suggesting that the CQDs fluorescence was dominated by carbon-core eigenstates. With the redshift of the PL emission, the A 2 of the CQDs increased from 8.48 % to 29.99 %, indicating a gradual increase in the effect of fluorescence induced by the surface defect states of the CQDs.

Since the prepared multicolor lignin-derived CQDs are complex in structure with abundant O- and N-containing groups on the surface, the photostability of the four CQDs was evaluated by investigating the effects of different pH values, radiation times, and interfering ions on the PL emission behavior. As shown in Fig. S2a, with 365 nm excitation, the PL intensity of the B-CQDs gradually decreases as the pH increases from 3 to 6, while it gradually increases as the pH continues to increase from 7 to 11, and the PL emission peaks are significantly blueshifted by 24 nm. In contrast to that of B-CQDs, the PL intensity of G-CQDs under 365 nm excitation decreases gradually as the pH increases from 3 to 11, and the PL emission peak gradually blueshifts by 12 nm. The PL intensities of Y-CQDs and R-CQDs at pH = 7–11 are slightly greater than those at pH = 3–6, and the PL emission peaks are only slightly blueshifted by 8 nm with increasing pH. The pH not only affects the PL intensity of the CQDs but also shifts the position of the PL emission peak. This may be due to the abundance of protonation and deprotonation groups of O-containing groups on the surface of the CQDs, whereas the change in the PL emission peak position with pH may be related to different surface defect states. Thus, the pH dependence of the PL emission behavior of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and R-CQDs decreases as the surface O content decreases. As shown in Fig. S2b, the four lignin-derived CQDs display negligible variation in their PL emission behavior after 0–12 h of UV light irradiation, and almost no photofading occurs. As shown in Fig. S2c, the PL intensity of the four lignin-based CQDs slightly varied, and the emission peaks did not shift under the interference of the anions Cl-, F-, NO3 –, and SO4 2- and the metal ions Na+, K+, and Mg2+. However, the presence of Fe3+ causes the PL intensity of the four CQDs to decrease sharply, and obvious fluorescence quenching occurs. This is because the abundant O-containing groups (–OH, –COOH) on the surface of the prepared lignin-derived CQDs act not only as electron donors but also as cross-linked ions at the edges of the CQDs to produce nonfluorescent complexes. Fe3+ ions with open d orbitals appear chelated in the presence of electron donors on the surface of the CQDs, thus leading to fluorescence quenching of the CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence mechanism of multicolor lignin-derived CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above results, the fluorescence mechanism of multicolor lignin-derived CQDs was investigated. As shown in Fig. 6 , the four lignin-derived CQDs are characterized by sp2-conjugated domains inside the carbon core and amorphous structures on the surface, and the PL behavior of the CQDs is affected by the synergistic effect of the graphitized and surface defect structures [39,40]. As the size increases from B-CQDs to R-CQDs, the degree of graphitization within the carbon cores and the content of C=O groups on the surfaces gradually increase. The increased graphitization within the carbon core of the CQDs allows for a wider range of π-electronic decomains, which causes a narrowing of Eg . There are various O-containing groups on the surface of the sp2 hybrid carbon core of CQDs, such as C-O-C, C-OH, and C=O, which tend to carry excess unbonded electrons and hybridize with the carbon core in different pathways. As a result, O-containing groups in the CQDs structure are capable of inducing multiple electron transition levels between the ground state and the excited state [40,41]. In addition, the O-related defect states formed by C=O in CODs as a strong electron acceptor in the sp2-conjugated system increase the significance of the multienergy electron transition. The presence of the C=O group is equivalent to the introduction of intermediate energy levels between π and π*, thus involving multipathway electronic transitions (n→π*). Upon excitation of the CQDs by varying excitation wavelengths, electrons in the ground state (π) transition to various energy levels in the excited state (π*) and subsequently relax to the lowest excited state (π*). The excited state electrons continue to relax to a variety of localized electronic states induced by O-containing groups (C-O-C, C-OH, and C=O) due to their instability and finally return to the ground state by radiative excursion and photon emission. Excited electrons converge to longer emission wavelengths through interband crossing, and the extra energy levels lead to multiple radiative recombination pathways returning to the ground state, thereby lowering the energy level of the LUMO and narrowing the Eg of the CQDs. As a result, the Eg from B-CQDs to R-CQDs gradually decreases as the graphitization degree and number of C=O surface groups gradually increase, followed by a corresponding gradual redshift of their PL emission peaks [32,38,42,43]. In addition, the luminescence QYs of multicolor lignin-derived CQDs mainly depend on the graphitic N (C-N) groups. The N atom can easily replace the C atom in the CQDs carbon skeleton to form a C–N structure, and its two isolated electrons can enhance the electron cloud density in the conjugated π domain and reduce the nonradiative recombination of CQDs, thus improving the PL intensity of the CQDs [26,28,44]. Consequently, the QYs of multicolor CQDs from lignin increase with increasing C–N group content.

The morphology of the pure BOI and CQD/BOI composites was characterized via SEM and HR-TEM. As shown in Fig. 7 a, pure BOI consists of nanoflowers with a diameter of approximately 1–2 μm composed of many very small nanosheets, and this distinctive morphology contributes to charge transfer and pollutant adsorption. As shown in Fig. 7b-e, BCQD/BOI, GCQD/BOI, YCQD/BOI, and BCQD/BOI showed similar regular three-dimensional layered nanoflowering microspheres with diameters of 1–2 μm. A small number of irregular nanoparticles grow on the nanosheets on the CQDs-modified BOIs with a slightly rough surface. Further detailed observation of the internal structure of the CQD/BOI was carried out using TEM and HR-TEM. As shown in Fig. 7f, the TEM image of RCQD/BOI shows floral spherical nanospheres, which is consistent with the SEM results. The HR-TEM image shows that tiny R-CQDs are attached to nanosheet surfaces without altering the shape of the BOI primary structure, where the structures with lattice spacings of 0.32 nm and 0.281 nm correspond to the (002) and (100) crystalline structures of RCQDs graphitic carbon and BOI, respectively [45,46]. In addition, the TEM maps in Fig. 7g-m show the distributions of C, N, O, Cl, I, and Bi on the microspheres of RCQD/BOI. These findings confirm the successful incorporation of CQDs on BOI in composite photocatalysts.

The crystalline structure, surface functional groups, and chemical compositions of the different composite photocatalysts were investigated using XRD, FT-IR, and XPS. As shown in Fig. 8 a, there are significant diffraction peaks at 28.5°, 32.1°, 45.9°, and 55.0° for pure BOI, matching the (102), (110), (200), and (212) crystal planes, respectively (JCPDS 40–0548) [45]. The characteristic peaks of BOI in the four CQD/BOI composite photocatalysts are visible, and all of them show a weak broad diffraction peak in the range of 10 ∼ 80°, with the peak position located at 26.7°, which corresponds to the graphitic carbon (002) crystal plane of the CQDs [37]. As shown in Fig. 8b, the stretching vibrations of pure BOI at 3420, 1593, 1380, 1066, 881, and 507 cm−1 are associated with the O–H, H-O–H, I-O-I, Bi-O, and I-O groups of adsorbed H2O, respectively [47,48]. The FT-IR spectra of the four CQD/BOI composite photocatalysts are relatively similar, exhibiting the characteristic peaks of BOI and surface CQDs in an integrated manner. Among them, there is a broad peak at 3440-3280 cm−1, which represents the stretching vibration of the O–H/N–H groups in the CQDs. The stretching vibrations at 2930, 1765, 1602, 1398, and 1121 cm−1 are associated with C–H, C=O, C=C, C-O, and C-N groups in the CQDs, respectively [36,49]. The stretching vibrations at 1593, 1380, 1065, 880, and 506 cm−1 are associated with the H-O–H, I-O-I, Bi-O, and I-O groups in BOI, respectively. As shown in Fig. S3, the XPS survey spectra indicate that the prepared BOI primarily consists of Bi, O, and I. The CQD/BOI composite photocatalyst primarily consists of C, N, Bi, O, and I, which is consistent with the TEM mapping results. The high-resolution XPS spectra indicate that C 1 s can be fitted to three peaks of C = O, C-O/C-N, and C–C/C=C. N 1 s can be fitted to two peaks of C-N and N–H [50]. The C 1 s and N 1 s peaks in the XPS spectra of the four CQD/BOI composite photocatalysts mainly originated from the modification of lignin-derived CQDs. These results further confirmed the successful preparation of the four CQD/BOI composite photocatalysts.

The optical properties and energy band structures of different photocatalysts were characterized using UV–vis DRS and VBXPS. As shown in Fig. 9 a, the light absorption regions of the BOI and the four CQD/BOI composites are mainly concentrated in the UV and visible regions, and the modification of the CQDs significantly enhanced the light absorption ability of the composite photocatalysts in the UV region. The light absorption boundary of BOI is approximately 625 nm and gradually redshifts from BCQD/BOI to RCQD/BOI. In particular, the R-CQDs modification significantly broadened the visible light region of the RCQD/BOI composite photocatalyst. This may be due to the interactions at the interface of the CQDs-modified BOI composites hindering light absorption, whereas the light absorption ability is not the only factor affecting the photocatalytic activity. As shown in Fig. 9b, the E g (calculated method in the Supporting Information) of BOI, BCQD/BOI, GCQD/BOI, YCQD/BOI, and RCQD/BOI are 1.67, 1.65, 1.64, 1.63, and 1.61 eV, respectively. The valence band potential (EVB ) and conduction band potential (ECB ) of a semiconductor can be determined from the XPS valence band spectrum (VBXPS). As shown in Fig. 9c, the EVB of BOI, BCQD/BOI, GCQD/BOI, YCQD/BOI, and RCQD/BOI are 0.70, 0.74, 0.79, 0.82, and 0.87 eV, respectively, and the ECB is −0.97, −0.91, −0.85, −0.81, and −0.74 eV, respectively. As shown in Fig. 10 , the incorporation of CQDs changes the energy band structure of the CQD/BOI composite photocatalysts. In comparison with that of pure BOI, the E g gradually narrows from BCQD/BOI to RCQD/BOI, and the light absorption region gradually broadens in the redshifted direction. The VB gradually decreases, and the ECB reflects the enhanced photocatalytic oxidation ability of the photocatalyst [18,51].

The electrochemical properties of the different photocatalysts were determined using PL, i-t, and EIS. As shown in Fig. 9d, compared with that of pure BOI, the PL intensity of the CQD/BOI composite photocatalyst sharply decreases, and the PL intensity decreases sequentially from BCQD/BOI to RCQD/BOI, with a slight redshift in the optimal emission peak position. This indicates that the modification of CQDs significantly inhibits the rapid recombination of BOI photogenerated carriers, in which the RCQD/BOI photogenerated carriers exhibit the lowest rate of recombination [52,53]. To validate the charge migration and separation efficiency of the prepared photocatalysts, the transient photocurrent response (i-t) was also determined. As shown in Fig. 9e, the composite photocatalysts show a pattern of photocurrent response as the Xe lamp is switched on and off, and the pure BOI presents the lowest photocurrent intensity over several Xe on–off cycles. This suggests that the photogenerated electrons and holes are prone to rapid recombination. The CQD/BOI composite photocatalysts displayed an enhanced photoresponse with a photocurrent intensity in the order of RCQD/BOI > YCQD/BOI > GCQD/BOI > BCQD/BOI. This demonstrates that the composition of CQDs with excellent electrical conductivity and BOIs significantly improves their electronic lifetime and efficient separation of e--h+ pairs. As shown in Fig. 9f, the interfacial charge migration rate of the photocatalyst was further illustrated by measuring the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) [54,55]. Among them, the impedance curve of RCQD/BOI has the smallest arc radius, indicating that it has the smallest charge transfer resistance and the fastest interfacial charge migration [56]. These photoelectrochemical results illustrate that the establishment of a tightly attached boundary between the CQDs and BOI accelerates the transfer of charge and inhibits the rapid recombination of e--h+ pairs.

The photocatalytic performance of CQDs-modified composites was evaluated by evaluating the degradation ability of TC. All photocatalysts reached the adsorption–desorption equilibrium of pollutants following 30 min of constant stirring in the dark. As shown in Fig. 11 a, the photocatalytic degradation of TC by pure BOI was limited, with a degradation efficiency of 70.9 % after 60 min of simulated sunlight. This is probably due to the strong photon scattering and fast charge recombination of monophase photocatalysts. The modification of the CQDs improved the photogenerated electron-hole separation efficiency of the composite photocatalysts, and the photocatalytic degradation efficiencies of BCQD/BOI, GCQD/BOI, YCQD/BOI, and RCQD/BOI for TC significantly increased to 78.7 %, 80.7 %, 83.3 %, and 94.7 %, respectively. As shown in Fig. 11b, RCQD/BOI has the highest kinetic degradation rate (0.03353 min−1), which is 2.0 times, 1.63 times, 1.55 times and 1.47 times greater than that of BOI (0.01669 min−1), BCQD/BOI (0.02055 min−1), GCQD/BOI (0.02170 min−1), and YCQD/BOI (0.02302 min−1), respectively. The RCQD/BOI composite photocatalyst exhibited the best photocatalytic activity, which may be attributed to the modification of RCQDs not only enhancing the UV absorption capability and broadening the visible absorption range but also showing the optimal separation efficiency for BOI photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which exhibited photocatalytic properties in agreement with the results of the photoelectrochemical performance characterization. As shown in Table S1, compared with other reported CQD-based photocatalysts for TC, RCQD/BOI has advantages in terms of photocatalytic degradation efficiency and rate. This strategy of applying CQDs derived from low-cost and environmentally friendly biomass to the field of water environment purification has certain commercial prospects. As shown in Fig. 11c, the photocatalytic TC degradation ability of BOI decreased dramatically from 70.9% to 28.7% after five cycles, whereas the photocatalytic TC degradation ability of the four prepared CQD/BOI composites was good (RCQD/BOI remained at 93.9%). This might be caused by the blockage of the active sites on the surface of pure BOI during reuse, and the attachment of CQDs as carriers to the surface of BOI in CQD/BOI contributes to the protection of the active sites. As shown in Fig. 11d, the XRD patterns of the RCQD/BOI composites were measured before and after the five-cycle experiments. In contrast, neither the appearance of new peaks nor significant shifts are observed in either of the XRD patterns, indicating that the chemical structure of the recycled RCQD/BOI is unaltered. These results reveal that the prepared RCQD/BOI has superior recyclability and stability.

The distribution of free radicals in the different photocatalysts was measured by EPR. CQDs significantly promoted the generation of free radicals in the composite photocatalysts (Fig. S4a, b). The signals of DMPO-
OH with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 and DMPO-
O2 − quadruple characteristic peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1:1 can be detected in the CQD/BOI spectra [57]. From BCQD/BOI to RCQD/BOI, the intensities of the characteristic peaks of DMPO-
OH and DMPO-
O2 − tend to increase sequentially. In particular, RCQD/BOI exhibited the best free radical signal peak intensity, which is in agreement with the experimental findings of the photocatalytic performance evaluation. The contribution of free radical components to the photocatalytic degradation reaction was further determined by free radical trapping experiments. The photocatalytic degradation efficiencies of RCQD/BOI for TC in the presence of IPA, BQ, and EDTA-2Na decreased from 94.7% to 57.1%, 74.6%, and 33.6%, respectively (Fig. S4c, d). The corresponding degradation rates decreased from 0.03353 to 0.00993, 0.01680 and 0.00559 min−1, respectively. It is evident that
OH radicals and h+ are the key active components of RCQD/BOI in the TC degradation process, followed by
O2 – radicals.

The possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism of the RCQD/BOI composite photocatalyst for the degradation of TC is proposed in Fig. 12 . The electrons and holes generated from BOI are located in the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB), respectively, under simulated sunlight irradiation. Normally, most photogenerated e--h+ pairs are prone to rapid recombination rather than being efficiently available for photocatalytic degradation reactions owing to the too-narrow Eg of BOI (1.67 eV). The conjugated π structure within the carbon core of the modified RCQDs in the RCQD/BOI composite photocatalysts has excellent electrical conductivity, and the electrons can migrate from the CB of the BOI to the CQDs surface. The internal electric field established at the close contact interface of RCQD/BOI dramatically improves the charge transfer rate and efficiently separates the photogenerated e+-h+ pairs inside the BOI [19,58]. As a result, extremely high e+ and h+ yields are promoted in the CB and VB of BOI, respectively, which allows direct use in photocatalytic reactions. The ECB of RCQD/BOI (−0.74 eV) is greater than that of O2/
O2 − (−0.13 eV vs NHE), while the EVB (0.87 eV) is less than that of H2O/
OH (2.38 eV vs. NHE). The energy band structure of RCQD/BOI suggests that the enriched electrons in its CB are capable of reducing dissolved O2 to
O2 − (O2 + e-→
O2 –). The produced
O2 − can further be reduced to
HO2 −, H2O2 and
OH through secondary reactions (
O2 –+ e- + H+→
HO2 –,
HO2 −+ H+→H2O2, H2O2 ↔ 2
OH) [59–61]. However, the EVB of RCQD/BOI is not sufficient to react with H2O/OH– to produce
OH, and the
OH radical in the RCQD/BOI photocatalytic system most likely originates from the secondary reaction of
O2 – [62,63]. The
O2 –,
OH, and h+ generated in the RCQD/BOI photocatalytic system are strongly oxidizing, and TC organic macromolecules are attacked by such free radicals and eventually breakdown into intermediates or CO2 and H2O. In addition, previous structural analysis confirmed that the surface of RCQDs contains abundant heteroatom (O, N, C) defect sites, which are beneficial for the activation of O2 to produce more active species [18,64]. The e-, h+, and radical-active species of the RCQD/BOI composite can be continuously refreshed under light radiation, allowing it to maintain efficient photocatalytic degradation performance. Hence, the modification of RCQDs not only strengthens the light absorption ability of the composites in the UV and visible regions but also enhances the photocatalytic degradation performance of RCQD/BOI by promoting the effective separation of e--h+ pairs and accelerating the charge transfer rate.

An environmentally friendly strategy was developed for synthesizing CQDs from lignin by an acidolysis-coupled hydrothermal method. By adjusting the N-containing dopants, four lignin-derived CQDs with bright fluorescence and color gradients from blue to red were successfully prepared, with the narrowing E g from 3.14 to 1.93 eV, corresponding to high fluorescence QYs of 10.8 %, 23.6 %, 21.8 %, and 20.5 %, respectively. The fluorescence mechanism of multicolor lignin-derived CQDs was also elucidated, revealing that the increased graphitization degree and C = O surface groups are crucial for the redshift of PL emission wavelengths. Additionally, the photodegradation efficiencies of the lignin-derived CQDs modified BOI composite photocatalysts decreased in the order of pure BOI (70.9%) < BCQD/BOI (78.7%) < GCQD/BOI (80.7%) < YCQD/BOI (83.3%) < RCQD/BOI (94.7%). RCQD/BOI exhibited the highest kinetic constant (0.03353 min−1), which was 2.0, 1.63, 1.55, and 1.47 times greater than that of BOI, BCQD/BOI, GCQD/BOI, and YCQD/BOI, respectively. The optimal composite photocatalyst (RCQD/BOI) exhibited an enhanced UV–visible absorption capacity, high-efficiency separation of photogenerated e--h+ pairs, and superior photocatalytic activity. The h+,
O2 –, and
OH radicals produced in the RCQD/BOI photocatalytic system are highly oxidizing, and TC organic macromolecules are attacked by such free radicals and eventually breakdown into intermediates or CO2 and H2O. This work has realized the controllable synthesis of multicolor biomass-derived CQDs, providing a green approach for the low-cost preparation of CQDs and sustainable application of CQDs-modified composite photocatalysts for the removal of antibiotic pollutants.
